meta.name: mneme-context-engine
meta.description: A Python library for injecting structured project memory into LLM API calls so outputs stay consistent with prior decisions.

items.title: Runtime
items.content: Python 3.11+. No support for earlier versions.

items.title: LLM provider
items.content: Anthropic Claude via the official anthropic SDK. No OpenAI, no litellm in v1.

items.title: Storage format
items.content: All project memory is stored in human-editable JSON files. No database in v1.

items.title: Core dependencies
items.content: anthropic and python-dotenv only. The dependency list must stay minimal.

items.title: Pipeline stages
items.content: load -> retrieve -> build_context -> call_llm -> evaluate. Each stage is a separate module with no cross-imports.

items.title: Extend current infrastructure before rebuilding
items.content: When adding capability, first ask whether an existing module can be extended. Do not introduce new abstractions or parallel pipelines unless the existing one is fundamentally incompatible with the requirement.

items.title: Keep v1 retrieval deterministic
items.content: Retrieval must be deterministic: same query and memory file must always return the same ranked list. No randomness, no learned weights, no embeddings in v1. Determinism is required for testability and trust.

items.title: Separate project memory from personal style
items.content: This library stores project-level decisions: architecture, constraints, tech stack choices. It does not store personal preferences, writing style, or user-specific context. That is a different product category.

items.title: Keep the MVP narrow and developer-friendly
items.content: Every feature added to v1 must answer yes to: 'Can a developer run this in under 5 minutes on a clean machine?' If it requires external services, accounts, or setup steps beyond pip install, it does not belong in v1.

items.title: Context injection goes in the system prompt
items.content: Retrieved memory is always injected via the system parameter of the Messages API. Do not append memory to the user turn or inject mid-conversation.

items.title: Dataclasses over Pydantic
items.content: Use stdlib dataclasses for all models. Pydantic adds validation capability but also a dependency and learning curve. If validation is needed later, add it explicitly rather than pulling in Pydantic by default.

items.title: Google-style docstrings with full type hints
items.content: All public functions and class attributes must have type hints. Docstrings on public symbols should use the Google style (Args, Returns, Raises sections). Private helpers can have shorter inline comments.

items.title: One class per module for pipeline stages
items.content: Each pipeline stage (MemoryStore, Retriever, ContextBuilder, LLMAdapter, Evaluator) lives in its own module and exports one primary class. Utility functions are allowed but should not grow into hidden second classes.

items.title: Keyword scoring for retrieval, not embeddings
items.content: Retrieval uses keyword-overlap scoring with tag boosting and per-item priority weighting. This is intentional: no ML dependency, fully deterministic, easy to debug. Embedding-based retrieval is deferred to v2 as an opt-in upgrade path.

items.title: Schema holds items and examples as separate lists
items.content: ProjectMemory contains two lists: items (facts, rules, preferences, etc.) and examples (decision records with task/decision/rationale). They serve different purposes in context injection and must not be merged into a single flat list.

items.title: Evaluator is deterministic, not an LLM judge
items.content: The evaluator checks rules and anti_patterns using string matching, not a secondary LLM call. This keeps evaluation fast, cheap, and auditable. An LLM-judge mode can be added later but must not replace the deterministic pass.

items.title: Do not use langchain
items.content: langchain must not be added as a dependency. It abstracts away the API surface this library is specifically designed to control, and it adds significant weight to the install.

items.title: Do not add agentic loops in v1
items.content: No tool-use, function calling, or multi-turn agent loops in this module. Mneme v1 is a single call-response pipeline. Agentic behaviour is a separate concern and a separate product layer.

items.title: Do not persist state between sessions in v1
items.content: There is no session state, no conversation history, no write-back to the memory file from runtime. The memory file is read-only at runtime. Stateful memory management is a v2+ concern.

items.title: Correct pipeline invocation
items.content: store = MemoryStore('examples/project_memory.json'); memory = store.load(); context = Retriever(memory).retrieve(query); system = ContextBuilder().build_system_context(memory, context); response = LLMAdapter().call(system=system, user=query)

examples.task: A contributor proposed adding sentence-transformers for semantic retrieval in v1.
examples.decision: Declined. Kept keyword scoring. Added a comment in retriever.py noting the embedding upgrade path.
examples.rationale: Sentence-transformers is a heavy ML dependency that breaks the 'pip install in 30 seconds' contract. Keyword scoring is good enough to demonstrate the concept and is fully deterministic. Embeddings are a v2 opt-in.

examples.task: Should project memory be stored per-user or per-project?
examples.decision: Per-project only. One JSON file per project, no user concept in v1.
examples.rationale: User-level memory conflates two different problems: project alignment (this library) and personal assistant style (a different product). Keeping the scope to project-level memory makes the value proposition clear and the data model simple.

examples.task: Should the evaluator call a second LLM to score alignment?
examples.decision: No. The evaluator uses deterministic string matching against rules and anti_patterns.
examples.rationale: An LLM judge adds latency, cost, and a second failure mode. For v1, the goal is demonstrating that memory injection reduces obvious violations. Deterministic evaluation is auditable and fast. LLM-judge can be added as an optional mode later.

examples.task: Should the context builder inject memory into the user turn instead of the system prompt?
examples.decision: Always the system prompt. The user turn is reserved for the actual task.
examples.rationale: Mixing project context into the user turn makes it harder for the model to distinguish task from constraints, and it pollutes the conversation history if multi-turn is added later. System prompt injection is cleaner and aligns with how Anthropic recommends structuring persistent context.

examples.task: A request came in to support OpenAI GPT-4o as an alternative LLM provider.
examples.decision: Deferred to v2. v1 ships with Anthropic only.
examples.rationale: Supporting multiple providers in v1 requires an abstraction layer (provider interface, config routing) that adds complexity without demonstrating the core value. The LLMAdapter is designed to be swappable - adding OpenAI support in v2 is a one-file change.

decisions.rationale: Avoid infra complexity and keep local-first. A human-editable JSON file is good enough for v1 and keeps the pip-install-in-30-seconds contract.
Same query + same memory file must always produce the same ranked list. Embeddings are a v2 opt-in.
Mneme v1 is a single call-response pipeline. Agentic behaviour is a separate concern.
